course.title: Lesson 23: One Man's Meat is Another Man's Poison
course.audioFilename: 23－One Man's Meat is Another Man's Poison.mp3

segments.text: Lesson 23.

translations.zh-Hans: 第23课。

segments.text: One Man's Meat is Another Man's Poison.

translations.zh-Hans: 甲之佳肴，乙之毒药。

segments.text: Listen to the tape, then answer the question below.

translations.zh-Hans: 听录音，然后回答下面的问题。

segments.text: What was it about snails that made the writer collect them for his friend on that day in particular?

translations.zh-Hans: 那天蜗牛有什么特别之处，使作者决定捉来送给朋友？

segments.text: People become quite illogical when they try to decide what can be eaten and what cannot be eaten.

translations.zh-Hans: 人们在决定什么能吃、什么不能吃时，会变得相当不合逻辑。

segments.text: If you lived in the Mediterranean, for instance, you would consider octopus a great delicacy.

translations.zh-Hans: 例如，如果你住在地中海地区，就会把章鱼视为绝佳美味。

segments.text: You would not be able to understand why some people find it repulsive.

translations.zh-Hans: 你会无法理解为什么有些人觉得它令人作呕。

segments.text: On the other hand, your stomach would turn at the idea of frying potatoes in animal fat, the normally accepted practice in many northern countries.

translations.zh-Hans: 另一方面，一想到用动物油炸土豆，你便会反胃，而这在许多北方国家却是公认的正常做法。

segments.text: The sad truth is that most of us have been brought up to eat certain foods, and we stick to them all our lives.

translations.zh-Hans: 可悲的事实是，我们大多数人从小被培养着吃某几种食物，并且一辈子都坚持如此。

segments.text: No creature has received more praise and abuse than the common garden snail.

translations.zh-Hans: 没有哪种生物像普通花园蜗牛一样，既受到如此多的赞美，又遭到如此多的辱骂。

segments.text: Cooked in wine, snails are a great luxury in various parts of the world.

translations.zh-Hans: 用酒烹制的蜗牛在世界许多地方都是珍馐。

segments.text: There are countless people who, ever since their early years, have learned to associate snails with food.

translations.zh-Hans: 无数人从幼年起便学会把蜗牛与食物联系起来。

segments.text: My friend, Robert, lives in a country where snails are despised.

translations.zh-Hans: 我的朋友罗伯特住在一个鄙视蜗牛的国家。

segments.text: As his flat is in a large town, he has no garden of his own.

translations.zh-Hans: 由于他的公寓位于大城市，他没有自己的花园。

segments.text: For years, he has been asking me to collect snails from my garden and take them to him.

translations.zh-Hans: 多年来，他一直让我从花园里捉些蜗牛带给他。

segments.text: The idea never appealed to me very much, but one day, after a heavy shower, I happened to be walking in my garden when I noticed a huge number of snails taking a stroll on some of my prized

translations.zh-Hans: 这个主意一直不太吸引我。不过一天大雨过后，我碰巧在花园里散步，发现大量蜗牛正在我珍爱的几株

segments.text: plants.

translations.zh-Hans: 植物上悠然爬行。

segments.text: Acting on a sudden impulse, I collected several dozen, put them in a paper bag, and took them to Robert.

translations.zh-Hans: 一时冲动之下，我捉了几十只，装进纸袋，带去给罗伯特。

segments.text: Robert was delighted to see me and equally pleased with my little gift.

translations.zh-Hans: 罗伯特见到我十分高兴，对我的小礼物也同样满意。

segments.text: I left the bag in the hall and Robert and I went into the living room where we talked for a couple of hours.

translations.zh-Hans: 我把袋子留在门厅，和罗伯特走进客厅，聊了几个小时。

segments.text: I had forgotten all about the snails when Robert suddenly said that I must stay to dinner.

translations.zh-Hans: 我早把蜗牛忘得一干二净，这时罗伯特突然说我一定要留下吃晚饭。

segments.text: Snails would, of course, be the main dish.

translations.zh-Hans: 当然，蜗牛将是主菜。

segments.text: I did not fancy the idea and I reluctantly followed Robert out of the room.

translations.zh-Hans: 我不喜欢这个主意，只好不情愿地跟着罗伯特走出房间。

segments.text: To our dismay, we saw that there were snails everywhere.

translations.zh-Hans: 令我们惊愕的是，到处都是蜗牛。

segments.text: They had escaped from the paper bag and had taken complete possession of the hall.

translations.zh-Hans: 它们已经从纸袋里逃出来，完全占领了门厅。

segments.text: I have never been able to look at a snail since then.

translations.zh-Hans: 从那以后，我再也无法正眼看一只蜗牛。
